statement.p: Town A and Town B are 700 miles apart. With a constant still-water speed, a boat traveled from Town A to Town B, and then back to Town A. During this whole trip, the river flew from Town A to Town B at 20 miles per hour. The whole trip took 12 hours. Answer the following question:
During this trip, the boats speed on still water was miles per hour.

solution.p: Assume that the boats still-water speed was x miles per hour.

Since the river flows from Town A to Town B at 20 miles per hour, the boat traveled from Town A toTown B (along the current) at x+20 miles per hour, and then traveled back from Town B to Town A (against the current) at x-20 miles per hour.
Using the formula time=\frac{distance}{speed}, we know the boat traveled from Town A to Town B in \frac{700}{x+20} hours; and it traveled back from Town B to Town A in \frac{700}{x-20} hours.
Its given that the whole trip took 12 hours, so we can write an equation and solve it:
When numbers are big, we use the quadratic formula to solve the quadratic equation:
In this situation, x=-3.33333333333333 is not a valid solution, since speed cannot be negative. x=120 is the only solution.
During this trip, the boats speed on still water was {120} miles per hour.
